news_summary: Today’s news highlights significant progress in human rights through the EU Parliament’s support for a renewed trade instrument focused on development, advancing a unified definition of rape based on consent across all EU countries, and addressing the impact of AI and algorithms on civil society and narratives.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière des avancées significatives en matière de droits de l'homme, grâce au soutien apporté par le Parlement européen à un instrument commercial renouvelé axé sur le développement, à la promotion d'une définition uniforme du viol fondée sur le consentement dans tous les pays de l'UE, ainsi qu'à la prise en compte de l'impact de l'intelligence artificielle et des algorithmes sur la société civile et les discours.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan avances significativos en materia de derechos humanos gracias al apoyo del Parlamento Europeo a un instrumento comercial renovado centrado en el desarrollo, al impulso de una definición unificada de la violación basada en el consentimiento en todos los países de la UE y al análisis del impacto de la inteligencia artificial y los algoritmos en la sociedad civil y las narrativas.

articles.title: Press release - Parliament endorses renewed EU trade instrument for development
articles.summary: On Tuesday, Parliament adopted the renewed regulation on the generalised system of preferences (GSP).Committee on International Trade Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
The updated rules, passed with 459 votes in favour, 127 against and 70 abstentions, allow vulnerable developing countries to export goods to the EU with low or no tariffs. Several international human rights and environmental conventions have been added to the list of international treaties that participating countries must ratify to benefit from trade preferences. These include the Paris Agreement, the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, and the Convention on the Rights of the Child.

Migrant readmission conditionality

MEPs managed to include a series of stricter criteria that will need to be fulfilled before GSP countries see their preferential tariffs withdrawn for continued non-cooperation on the readmission of irregular migrants. These criteria include a longer evaluation procedure and mandatory engagement of at least 12 months with the countries concerned. There will also be a two-year delay for the least developed countries in the application of the readmission conditionality.

Rice

To do more to protect the sensitive EU rice sector, Parliament’s negotiators ensured that automatic safeguards will be triggered once a 45% surge of rice imports on an average of 10 years is reached.

Quote

Bernd Lange (S&D, DE), Chair of the Committee on International Trade, and rapporteur, said:

"Today we deliver on the EU's flagship development scheme that benefits more than 2 billion people in over 60 countries. By providing near-duty-free and quota-free access to the EU market for another 10 years, the EU is redoubling its efforts to be a trustworthy, predictable and durable partner for the developing world.

Two issues prolonged the negotiations. On readmissions, Council moved considerably to meet Parliament’s concerns, creating a balanced system with clear guardrails and a differentiated system for least developed countries. In practice, this conditionality will be very hard to trigger. On rice safeguards, we now have a system that will be triggered automatically in the event of excessive volumes of rice imports.

Ultimately, this system remains what it must be—a driver of sustainable growth, poverty reduction, and fair globalization. Trade is not an end in itself. It is a tool and we have now improved it. This is particularly significant at a time when China is intent in expanding its trade ties and the US is raising trade barriers for the developing world."

Next steps

Once formally adopted by the Council of the EU, the legislation will be signed and published in the Official Journal of the EU. It will then enter into force and apply for a period of 10 years.

Background

The generalised system of preferences (GSP) has been the EU's preferential trade arrangement with developing countries since 1971. It offers developing countries reduced duties when exporting to the EU with the aim of eradicating poverty, promoting sustainable development, and better integrating these countries in the world economy. The GSP system covers more than 60 countries and 2 billion people around the world. Parliament voted in October 2023 to extend current rules after talks with the Council on the new rules were paused.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the EU Parliament's adoption of a renewed trade regulation (GSP) that benefits over 2 billion people in more than 60 developing countries by allowing low or no tariffs on exports to the EU. It includes new human rights and environmental treaty requirements, promotes sustainable growth, poverty reduction, and fair globalization, and introduces balanced criteria for migrant readmission conditionality. The impact is broad, significant, and well-detailed, focusing on a major policy with tangible benefits for developing countries.
articles.personality_title: EU Parliament renews trade rules to support over 2 billion people in developing countries
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Union has a system called the Generalised System of Preferences (GSP) that helps developing countries sell their goods to EU countries with lower taxes or no taxes. This system has been in place since 1971 to encourage growth and reduce poverty in poorer countries.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the European Parliament approved new rules to renew the GSP for another 10 years. These updated rules passed with 459 votes in favor. The new agreement adds important requirements: countries must follow key international human rights and environmental agreements, like the Paris Climate Agreement and the Convention on the Rights of the Child, to benefit from the trade preferences.

The Parliament also set stricter conditions for withdrawing trade benefits if a country does not cooperate on sending back irregular migrants. There will be a longer review process and a delay of two years before these rules apply to the poorest countries. Additionally, special protections were added to prevent too much rice from being imported into the EU at once, protecting European rice farmers.

**Impact** – This renewal supports more than 2 billion people in over 60 developing countries by making it easier and cheaper for them to sell products in Europe. By including human rights and environmental treaties, the EU is encouraging fair and responsible trade. The new rules carefully balance support with clear conditions, especially on migration and protecting sensitive EU industries like rice farming.

**What's next step** – The new rules will now be reviewed and officially approved by the EU Council. After that, they will be published and come into effect for 10 years, allowing developing countries to continue benefiting from easier access to the EU market.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU has renewed its trade system to help billions in developing countries export more easily while promoting human rights, environmental care, and fair migration rules.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen renouvelle les règles commerciales pour soutenir plus de 2 milliards de personnes dans les pays en développement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Union européenne dispose d'un système appelé Système généralisé de préférences (SGP) qui aide les pays en développement à vendre leurs produits en Europe avec des taxes réduites ou nulles. Ce système existe depuis 1971 pour encourager la croissance et réduire la pauvreté.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Mardi, le Parlement européen a approuvé de nouvelles règles pour renouveler le SGP pour 10 ans. Ces règles ont été adoptées avec 459 voix pour. Le nouvel accord demande aux pays de respecter des traités internationaux importants sur les droits humains et l'environnement, comme l'Accord de Paris sur le climat et la Convention relative aux droits de l'enfant, pour bénéficier des préférences commerciales.

Le Parlement a aussi fixé des conditions plus strictes avant de retirer les avantages commerciaux si un pays ne coopère pas sur le renvoi des migrants irréguliers. Il y aura un examen plus long et un délai de deux ans pour les pays les plus pauvres. De plus, des protections spéciales ont été ajoutées pour éviter une trop grande importation de riz dans l'UE, afin de protéger les agriculteurs européens.

**Impact** – Ce renouvellement soutient plus de 2 milliards de personnes dans plus de 60 pays en développement en facilitant et en rendant moins coûteuse la vente de leurs produits en Europe. En incluant les traités sur les droits humains et l'environnement, l'UE encourage un commerce juste et responsable. Les nouvelles règles équilibrent soutien et conditions claires, notamment sur la migration et la protection des industries sensibles comme la riziculture.

**Prochaine étape** – Les nouvelles règles seront maintenant examinées et officiellement approuvées par le Conseil de l'UE. Ensuite, elles seront publiées et entreront en vigueur pour 10 ans, permettant aux pays en développement de continuer à bénéficier d'un meilleur accès au marché européen.

**Phrase clé** – L'UE a renouvelé son système commercial pour aider des milliards de personnes dans les pays en développement à exporter plus facilement tout en promouvant les droits humains, la protection de l'environnement et des règles justes sur la migration.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento de la UE renueva normas comerciales para apoyar a más de 2 mil millones de personas en países en desarrollo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Unión Europea tiene un sistema llamado Sistema Generalizado de Preferencias (SGP) que ayuda a los países en desarrollo a vender sus productos en Europa con impuestos bajos o sin impuestos. Este sistema existe desde 1971 para fomentar el crecimiento y reducir la pobreza.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Parlamento Europeo aprobó nuevas reglas para renovar el SGP por otros 10 años. Estas reglas fueron aprobadas con 459 votos a favor. El nuevo acuerdo exige que los países cumplan con importantes tratados internacionales sobre derechos humanos y medio ambiente, como el Acuerdo de París y la Convención sobre los Derechos del Niño, para beneficiarse de las preferencias comerciales.

El Parlamento también estableció condiciones más estrictas para retirar beneficios comerciales si un país no coopera en la devolución de migrantes irregulares. Habrá un proceso de revisión más largo y un retraso de dos años para que estas reglas apliquen en los países más pobres. Además, se añadieron protecciones especiales para evitar que se importe demasiado arroz a la UE, protegiendo a los agricultores europeos.

**Impacto** – Esta renovación apoya a más de 2 mil millones de personas en más de 60 países en desarrollo al facilitar y abaratar la venta de sus productos en Europa. Al incluir tratados sobre derechos humanos y medio ambiente, la UE promueve un comercio justo y responsable. Las nuevas reglas equilibran el apoyo con condiciones claras, especialmente sobre migración y protección de industrias sensibles como la producción de arroz.

**Próximo paso** – Las nuevas reglas serán revisadas y aprobadas oficialmente por el Consejo de la UE. Luego se publicarán y entrarán en vigor por 10 años, permitiendo que los países en desarrollo sigan beneficiándose de un mejor acceso al mercado europeo.

**Frase clave** – La UE ha renovado su sistema comercial para ayudar a miles de millones en países en desarrollo a exportar más fácilmente mientras promueve derechos humanos, cuidado ambiental y reglas justas sobre migración.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an open gateway shaped like the European Union flag, welcoming stylized bundles of diverse goods wrapped in natural fabrics being carried by abstract figures representing developing countries; nearby, balanced scales hold treaties symbolized by glowing scrolls labeled with icons for human rights and environmental care, while a protective rice stalk arch subtly frames the scene, all rendered in soft, earthy colors with gentle light conveying partnership and sustainable growth.

articles.title: Press release - Rape must be defined based on the absence of consent in all EU countries
articles.summary: Parliament wants the Commission to propose legislation establishing a common definition of rape based on freely given, informed, and revocable consent.Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender EqualityCommittee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
In a report adopted on Tuesday with 447 votes for, 160 against, and 43 abstentions, MEPs call on member states that still rely on force- or violence-based definitions of rape to align their laws with international standards (including the Istanbul Convention, ratified by the EU in 2023). They also demand adequate support and protection for victims and survivors across the EU. Parliament says silence, lack of resistance, the absence of a “no”, previous consent, past sexual conduct, or any current or previous relationship must not be interpreted as consent.

A new legal framework

MEPs say consent must be assessed in context, including in cases involving violence, threats, the abuse of power, fear, intimidation, unconsciousness, intoxication, chemical submission, sleep, illness, disability or vulnerability. They argue trauma responses (such as the “freeze” or the “fawn” responses) must be reflected in legislation and judicial practice and reiterate their demand for gender-based violence to be added to the charges considered as EU crimes.

A victim-centred approach

Only consent-based rape legislation can ensure access to justice, Parliament says. MEPs say the EU needs an intersectional and victim-centred approach, including the provision of immediate medical care, sexual and reproductive healthcare, safe and legal abortion, trauma care, psychological support, and legal assistance. They also want free specialist support services, including 24-hour crisis centres offering medical, psychological and legal support.

Training, education and awareness

The report calls for regular and tailored mandatory training for professionals likely to come into contact with rape victims, including law enforcement officers, judges, prosecutors, lawyers, healthcare professionals and frontline workers. MEPs want the Commission to present EU guidelines in 2026 on comprehensive sexuality and relationship education, as well as for EU-wide awareness campaigns on consent, relationships, sexual integrity and bodily autonomy, and for action against rape myths, anti-gender content, and incel propaganda online.

Quotes

Civil Liberties Committee rapporteur Evin Incir (S&D, Sweden) said: “It is both morally and legally unacceptable that women are not protected by ‘only yes means yes’ legislation across the EU. We have been calling for a common European definition of rape for years, and although the Council prevented its inclusion as part of the directive on combating violence against women, more and more governments are recognising the need for this approach – since 2023, France, Finland, Luxembourg and the Netherlands have introduced consent-based laws. Momentum is with us: it’s time to deliver a common European definition of rape based on the absence of freely given and revocable consent.”

Women’s Rights Committee rapporteur Joanna Scheuring-Wielgus (S&D, Poland) said: “One in three women in the EU has experienced gender-based violence. One in twenty has been raped. Thanks to brave women like Gisèle Pelicot, there are growing calls for action. But we know that in our communities there are countless victims, many who will never see justice. Parliament is speaking up for justice by calling on the Commission to propose legislation, so we can actively improve the situation for women, moving beyond outdated laws to guarantee them the same level of protection across the EU.”

At 17.00 CEST on Tuesday 28 April, the rapporteurs will hold a press conference in Strasbourg, with the possibility of remote participation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant legislative push within the EU to establish a common, consent-based definition of rape, aiming to harmonize laws, enhance victim protection, and improve access to justice across all member states. This represents a meaningful advancement in human rights and gender equality with broad societal impact, supported by detailed context on legal, medical, and educational measures.
articles.personality_title: EU Parliament pushes for common consent-based definition of rape
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Across the European Union, laws defining rape vary. Some countries still require proof of force or violence, which can make it harder for victims to get justice. The EU wants to change this by creating a shared, clear definition based on consent.

**What happened** – On April 25, 2026, the European Parliament voted to urge the European Commission to propose a law that defines rape as sex without freely given, informed, and revocable consent. The vote was 447 in favor, 160 against, and 43 abstentions. The Parliament also called for better support for victims, including medical care, psychological help, and legal assistance.

**Impact** – This is important because it shifts the focus from proving violence to proving lack of consent, which better reflects how victims experience these crimes. It also recognizes that silence or fear does not mean consent. Countries like France and the Netherlands already have such laws, and the EU wants all members to follow. This change could make it easier for victims to get justice and receive the help they need.

**What's next step** – The European Commission is expected to draft new legislation based on this call. In 2026, the EU will also work on education programs about consent and relationships, and training for police, judges, and healthcare workers. Awareness campaigns will aim to fight myths about rape and support victims.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU Parliament is moving to create a clear, consent-based legal definition of rape to improve justice and support for victims across all member countries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen réclame une définition commune du viol basée sur le consentement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans l'Union européenne, les lois sur le viol sont différentes selon les pays. Certains exigent encore la preuve de violence, ce qui complique l'accès à la justice pour les victimes. L'UE souhaite harmoniser ces lois avec une définition claire basée sur le consentement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 25 avril 2026, le Parlement européen a voté pour demander à la Commission européenne de proposer une loi définissant le viol comme un acte sexuel sans consentement libre, éclairé et rétractable. Le vote a réuni 447 voix pour, 160 contre et 43 abstentions. Le Parlement a aussi demandé un meilleur soutien aux victimes, comme des soins médicaux, un accompagnement psychologique et une aide juridique.

**Impact** – Ce changement est important car il met l'accent sur l'absence de consentement plutôt que sur la preuve de violence, ce qui correspond mieux à la réalité des victimes. Il reconnaît que le silence ou la peur ne signifient pas consentement. Des pays comme la France et les Pays-Bas ont déjà adopté ces lois, et l'UE veut que tous les membres les suivent. Cela pourrait faciliter la justice et l'aide aux victimes.

**Prochaine étape** – La Commission européenne va préparer une nouvelle législation. En 2026, l'UE développera aussi des programmes d'éducation sur le consentement et les relations, ainsi que des formations pour la police, les juges et les professionnels de santé. Des campagnes de sensibilisation lutteront contre les idées fausses sur le viol et soutiendront les victimes.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen agit pour une définition claire du viol basée sur le consentement, afin d'améliorer la justice et le soutien aux victimes dans tous les pays membres.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa una definición común de violación basada en el consentimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la Unión Europea, las leyes sobre violación son diferentes en cada país. Algunos todavía exigen demostrar fuerza o violencia, lo que dificulta que las víctimas obtengan justicia. La UE quiere cambiar esto con una definición clara basada en el consentimiento.

**Qué pasó** – El 25 de abril de 2026, el Parlamento Europeo votó para pedir a la Comisión Europea que proponga una ley que defina la violación como sexo sin consentimiento libre, informado y reversible. La votación fue 447 a favor, 160 en contra y 43 abstenciones. También pidieron mejor apoyo para las víctimas, como atención médica, ayuda psicológica y asistencia legal.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque cambia el enfoque de probar violencia a probar la falta de consentimiento, reflejando mejor la experiencia de las víctimas. Se reconoce que el silencio o el miedo no significan consentimiento. Países como Francia y los Países Bajos ya tienen estas leyes, y la UE quiere que todos los miembros las adopten. Esto podría facilitar que las víctimas obtengan justicia y ayuda.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que la Comisión Europea prepare la nueva ley. En 2026, la UE también trabajará en programas educativos sobre consentimiento y relaciones, y en la formación de policías, jueces y profesionales de salud. Se lanzarán campañas para combatir mitos sobre la violación y apoyar a las víctimas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo avanza para crear una definición legal clara de violación basada en el consentimiento, mejorando la justicia y el apoyo a las víctimas en todos los países miembros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of interlocking hands forming a protective circle around a softly glowing, translucent dove symbolizing consent and freedom, set against a calm background of gentle, natural earth tones and subtle light rays that evoke hope, unity, and support within a harmonious abstract space.

articles.title: New tech, new rules: Narrative and civil society in the age of AI and algorithms
articles.summary: Overall, while the surveillance, abuses and huge concentrations and imbalances of power that AI and other new technologies enable are very worrying, we found a good deal of encouragement in these case studies.
articles.content:
By Brett Davidson

This post is part of Global Voices’ April 2026 Spotlight series, “Human perspectives on AI.” This series will offer insight into how AI is being used in global majority countries, how its use and implementation are affecting individual communities, what this AI experiment might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

Technology such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and algorithm-driven platforms is having unprecedented impacts on almost every aspect of life, from academia to employment to the environment. When it comes to social justice, democracy and human rights, things look bleak: algorithms are channeling us into ideological echo-chambers, while AI has taken surveillance and data extraction to alarming new levels. At the same time, social media platforms enable civil society organizations to reach audiences and tell stories in powerful new ways, while AI allows people to do research and message testing they would never otherwise be able to afford.

As part of a recent project called “New Tech, New Rules,” the International Resource for Impact and Storytelling (IRIS), with support from Luminate and the Open Society Foundations, commissioned 10 case studies from organizations and researchers across the Global Majority, to hear how this new technology is affecting them and their work, and how they are adapting and adjusting. The studies came from across Latin America and the Caribbean, the Arab region, Nigeria, Tunisia, India and Hong Kong.

Each case study is incredibly rich and interesting in itself (and some of their authors are writing about them for Global Voices) — but, looking across them, we were able to discern several interesting patterns that perhaps resonate and offer lessons for others.

A toolkit of tactics: Co-opting, countering and innovating

Across the case studies, we saw three clear ways in which civil society is responding to the tech-infused authoritarian context: through Co-opting, Countering and Innovating.

Some organizations are co-opting culture and technology for their own purposes: for example, Fogo Cruzado in Brazil is collaborating with the UK-based Future Narratives Lab to use AI to test messages aimed at reducing public support for police violence in favelas.

Others are focused on countering and resisting tech-driven surveillance and digital violence, such as Derechos Digitales, which is using social media to campaign and mobilise opposition against the widespread use of facial recognition technologies by authorities in Brazil and Chile.

Still others are innovating, finding new forms and approaches to journalism and to narrative civic engagement. An example of the former is Alharaca, a group of feminist journalists from El Salvador working in exile and experimenting with getting offline and slowing down rather than giving in to the tech-driven pressure to speed up. They are bringing audience members together in person and trying out new storytelling approaches, such as board games and immersive sound installations. When it comes to civil society, activists in Hong Kong are adapting to pervasive AI-driven surveillance by using humor, embedding meaning in seemingly innocuous terminology and working through short-lived, temporary organizations.

Of course, co-opting technology, countering it, and innovating in the face of it are not mutually exclusive. Fogo Cruzado’s co-optation of the research possibilities AI offers is, of course, hugely innovative. It is only through narrative and organizational innovation that activists in places like Hong Kong and Tunisia are able to counter and resist state repression. Nevertheless, they are also distinct approaches, and we believe this trio of tactics serves as a potentially useful menu for organizations to consider as they navigate our current moment.

From the hyperlocal to the transnational

Several of the case studies foreground a turn to the hyperlocal as a way to prioritize grassroots issues and voices sidelined by national media and politics, as well as to sidestep the scrutiny of authorities mostly focused on the national stage. Shifting the frame from one that places national politics in the center, what was previously thought of as marginal becomes central: hyperlocal stories, news and politics are where it’s at. This is where politics intersects with people’s lives, where connection and power-building can happen outside of surveilled tech platforms, where issues intersect, where audiences and media creators overlap, and where change seems possible.

At the same time, there is a turn to the supra-national, looking at trends and connecting the dots of political developments beyond the nation-state, and as a way of sharing lessons and building cross-border solidarity. Just as authoritarians connect and learn from one another across borders, just as technology knows no borders, so civil society actors realize they need to connect across boundaries and borders to make meaning and build power.

Flexibility is everything

In a fast-changing environment, where techno-authoritarianism is both continually innovating and intentionally disrupting and overwhelming, being flexible and adaptable is an essential strategy. While this is apparent across all of the case studies, perhaps the clearest example comes from Hong Kong, where the case study authors highlight the importance of ephemeral infrastructures that can appear, dissolve and reappear or reconfigure themselves as needed. These arrangements — micro-groups, informal collectives, rotating convenors, volunteer networks — sustain momentum while avoiding singular points of vulnerability. This is also a really important lesson for funders to note – both resilience and impact depend on the ability to anticipate and pivot at short notice, and funding arrangements need to build this in.

It takes a constellation

No single organization can do everything; no one can achieve systemic impact acting alone. As our case studies illustrate, robust, decentralized and collaborative networks are necessary, both within countries and across borders. Narrative workers and civil society actors are actively forming alliances and seeking assistance and expertise from organizations and peers from beyond their fields to bolster their narrative efforts, strengthen their advocacy campaigns, and guard themselves against digital attacks and surveillance. Funding strategies in turn should proactively support what the Polis Project calls “the infrastructure of resistance.”

The interconnectedness of narrative, technology and power

Across the 10 case studies, we see three elements in constant interaction, each inseparable from the others: narrative, technology and politics. Civil society actors are narrative workers: narrative is upstream of technology, and culture and meaning-making constitute THE key terrain of struggle right now. They are technologists: operating with, on and through current and emergent technology to build power and engage in the struggle over culture, narrative and meaning, constantly adapting and innovating, always balancing technology’s possibilities and affordances with its risks, limitations and harms. They are political actors, organizers and power-builders: making meaning both about and with technology, in the interests of social justice and democracy.

Overall, while the surveillance, abuses, and huge concentrations and imbalances of power that AI and other new technologies enable are very worrying, we found a good deal of encouragement in these case studies. Operating in hostile territory, using tools owned by so-called “broligarchs” and authoritarians, social justice-focused advocates and activists, journalists and storytellers around the world are successfully adapting and employing technology and culture to build people power in the interests of democracy and social justice.

Brett Davidson is founder and principal at Wingseed LLC, and works with the International Resource for Impact and Storytelling (IRIS) as lead for narrative infrastructure building. Previously he was Director of Media and Narratives at the Open Society Public Health Program, and before that worked in civil society and a radio journalist in South Africa. He writes about narrative change and listening as a political act.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on civil society organizations across multiple countries innovatively using AI and technology to counter authoritarian surveillance and promote social justice and democracy. It highlights broad, meaningful impacts on human rights and democracy through adaptive, collaborative strategies, providing detailed case studies and lessons that inspire hope and resilience.
articles.personality_title: How civil society uses AI to fight surveillance and promote justice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – New technologies like Artificial Intelligence (AI) and algorithms are changing many parts of life, including how people share stories, fight for rights, and face challenges from governments using tech to watch and control them.

**What happened** – A project called “New Tech, New Rules” collected 10 stories from groups in places like Brazil, India, Hong Kong, and Nigeria. These groups showed how they use AI and technology in three ways: to co-opt or use tech for their own goals, to resist harmful surveillance, and to create new ways of telling stories and organizing.

**Impact** – The stories reveal that local groups are finding creative ways to protect themselves and share their messages despite tough conditions. Some use AI to test messages against police violence, others fight against facial recognition tech, and some bring people together offline with games and art. These strategies help keep people connected, build power, and resist control.

**What's next step** – These groups plan to keep adapting quickly, working together across borders, and using flexible methods like temporary groups to stay safe. Funders and supporters are encouraged to help by providing flexible resources that allow these groups to respond to fast-changing challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Around the world, civil society groups are creatively using AI and technology to resist surveillance and promote social justice by adapting, collaborating, and innovating.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment la société civile utilise l’IA pour lutter contre la surveillance et promouvoir la justice
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les nouvelles technologies comme l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et les algorithmes changent de nombreux aspects de la vie, notamment la façon dont les gens racontent des histoires, défendent leurs droits et font face aux gouvernements qui utilisent la technologie pour surveiller et contrôler.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un projet appelé « Nouvelles technologies, nouvelles règles » a rassemblé 10 témoignages de groupes dans des pays comme le Brésil, l’Inde, Hong Kong et le Nigeria. Ces groupes ont montré comment ils utilisent l’IA et la technologie de trois manières : pour détourner la technologie à leurs fins, résister à la surveillance néfaste, et inventer de nouvelles façons de raconter des histoires et s’organiser.

**Impact** – Ces histoires montrent que les groupes locaux trouvent des moyens créatifs de se protéger et de faire passer leurs messages malgré des conditions difficiles. Certains utilisent l’IA pour tester des messages contre la violence policière, d’autres combattent la reconnaissance faciale, et certains rassemblent les gens hors ligne avec des jeux et de l’art. Ces stratégies aident à maintenir les liens, renforcer le pouvoir et résister au contrôle.

**Prochaines étapes** – Ces groupes comptent continuer à s’adapter rapidement, collaborer au-delà des frontières, et utiliser des méthodes flexibles comme des groupes temporaires pour rester en sécurité. Les financeurs sont encouragés à fournir des ressources flexibles pour permettre cette réactivité.

**Une phrase à retenir** – Partout dans le monde, les groupes de la société civile utilisent de manière créative l’IA et la technologie pour résister à la surveillance et promouvoir la justice sociale grâce à l’adaptation, la collaboration et l’innovation.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la sociedad civil usa la IA para combatir la vigilancia y promover la justicia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las nuevas tecnologías como la inteligencia artificial (IA) y los algoritmos están cambiando muchas áreas de la vida, incluyendo cómo las personas cuentan historias, luchan por sus derechos y enfrentan a gobiernos que usan tecnología para vigilarlos y controlarlos.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto llamado “Nueva tecnología, nuevas reglas” reunió 10 relatos de grupos en países como Brasil, India, Hong Kong y Nigeria. Estos grupos mostraron cómo usan la IA y la tecnología de tres formas: para aprovechar la tecnología para sus propios fines, resistir la vigilancia dañina y crear nuevas formas de contar historias y organizarse.

**Impacto** – Las historias muestran que los grupos locales encuentran formas creativas de protegerse y compartir sus mensajes a pesar de las dificultades. Algunos usan IA para probar mensajes contra la violencia policial, otros luchan contra el reconocimiento facial, y algunos reúnen a las personas fuera de línea con juegos y arte. Estas estrategias ayudan a mantener conexiones, construir poder y resistir el control.

**Próximo paso** – Estos grupos planean seguir adaptándose rápido, trabajando juntos más allá de las fronteras y usando métodos flexibles como grupos temporales para mantenerse seguros. Se anima a los financiadores a apoyar con recursos flexibles que permitan responder a desafíos cambiantes.

**Una frase clave** – En todo el mundo, los grupos de la sociedad civil usan creativamente la IA y la tecnología para resistir la vigilancia y promover la justicia social mediante la adaptación, colaboración e innovación.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting depicting a network of glowing, interwoven threads connecting diverse symbolic elements: a stylized open book representing storytelling and narrative, abstracted digital nodes symbolizing AI and algorithms, and a mosaic of small, diverse community figures united in collaborative circles—set against a softly glowing, warm-toned backdrop that conveys resilience, adaptation, and collective power-building.

articles.title: Press release - EP TODAY
articles.summary: Tuesday 28 April Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
2028-2034 EU budget

Following a debate with Budget Commissioner Piotr Serafin from 9.00, Parliament will vote at noon on its position on the EU’s next long-term budget – the multi-annual financial framework (MFF), including a breakdown of the amounts MEPs want to allocate to each EU funding programme. Parliament will then be ready to start negotiations with the Council on the regulation setting the structure and main figures for the 2028-2034 budget, which requires Parliament’s consent to be approved.

Parliament’s president, Roberta Metsola, and lead MEPs will hold a press conference after the vote at 14.00, in the Daphne Caruana Galizia press conference room (WEISS N -1/201).

Eszter ZALÁN

(+32) 477 99 20 73

@EP_Budgets

Accountability and justice for Ukraine

From around 19.00, Defence Commissioner Andrius Kubilius will join MEPs to discuss the need to ensure Russia is held accountable for its relentless attacks on Ukrainian civilians. Parliament will vote on a resolution on the subject on Thursday.

Earlier, at noon, MEPs will vote on whether to apply the urgent procedure to a proposal for the EU to join the convention establishing an International Claims Commission for Ukraine. The initiative forms part of Europe's collective efforts to ensure accountability and justice for Ukraine. If the vote passes, MEPs will vote on the substantive content of the proposal on Thursday.

Viktor ALMQVIST

(+32) 470 88 29 42

Snjezana KOBESCAK SMODIS

(+32) 470 96 08 19

@EP_ForeignAff

Consent-based legal definition of rape

Following their debate yesterday evening, MEPs will vote at noon on a report by the Civil Liberties and Women’s Rights Committees calling for legislation establishing a common definition of rape based on the absence of freely given, informed and revocable consent. A press conference with the rapporteurs is scheduled for 17.00.

Kyriakos KLOSIDIS

(+32) 470 96 47 35

Polona TEDESKO

(+32) 470 88 42 82

@EP_Justice

@EP_GenderEqual

New legislation to protect cats and dogs

MEPs will vote at noon today on the first ever EU standards for the breeding, housing, traceability, import and handling of cats and dogs, agreed with EU member states at the end of last year. Under the new law, all dogs and cats kept in the EU, including those in private ownership, will eventually need to be identifiable with a microchip and be registered in interoperable national databases.

Eliana CAPRETTI

(+32) 473 33 25 82

@EP_Agriculture

In brief

Parliament-Commission working relations. Roberta Metsola and European Commission president Ursula von der Leyen will sign the new framework agreement for cooperation between their two institutions today at noon. Building on an existing 2010 agreement, the text reinforces parliamentary scrutiny of legislative and international processes, and strengthens the Commission’s political accountability.

State of the rule of law and fundamental rights in the EU. From around 17.00, Commissioner Michael McGrath will join MEPs to discuss the situation of fundamental rights in the EU in 2024 and 2025, and the Commission’s 2025 rule of law report. Parliament will vote on both reports tomorrow. A press conference on the rule of law will take place at 15.30 today and one on fundamental rights tomorrow morning at 10.30.

Generalised scheme of preferences. At noon, MEPs will vote to endorse rules for the EU’s preferential trade arrangement with developing countries, which allows them to export goods to the EU with low or zero tariffs.

Digital Markets Act. In a debate from around 15.45,MEPs will be quizzing Commissioner Teresa Ribera on the need for quicker compliance proceedings and closer scrutiny of AI-driven search tools and cloud services in the context of the Digital Markets Act review. They will vote on a resolution on Thursday.

Armenia. As the country’s June parliamentary elections draw nearer, amid fears of external interference, MEPs and Commissioner Andrius Kubilius will discuss the need to reinforce Armenia’s democratic resilience, in a debate from around 20.00. Parliament will vote on a resolution on Thursday.

Parliamentary oversight of 2024 budget. From 13.00, MEPs and Piotr Serafin will hold a joint debate on the way the Commission and other EU institutions managed the EU budget in 2024. They will vote on whether to grant discharge tomorrow.

Votes

At noon, MEPs will also vote, among others, on:

a single EU method for calculating transport sector greenhouse gas emissions;

Parliament’s priorities for the 2027 budget;

an own-initiative report calling for minimum standards for financial influencers;

a report to promote sustainable tourism in the EU;

the 2024 annual report on protecting the EU’s financial interests; and

the 2024 annual report on the activities of the European Investment Bank.

Live coverage of the plenary session can be found on Parliament’s webstreaming site and on EbS+.

For detailed information on the session, please also see our newsletter.

Find more information regarding plenary.
articles.reasoning: The article details multiple significant EU parliamentary actions with broad societal impact, including establishing a consent-based legal definition of rape, new legislation to protect cats and dogs, and efforts to ensure accountability for attacks on Ukrainian civilians. These actions represent meaningful progress in human rights, animal welfare, and justice, benefiting large populations across the EU and beyond.
articles.personality_title: EU Parliament advances new laws on consent, animal protection, and Ukraine accountability
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Parliament is meeting to discuss and vote on several important issues affecting people and animals across the European Union. These include the next EU budget, legal protections, and justice efforts related to the conflict in Ukraine.

**What happened** – On April 28, Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) voted on a report calling for a clear legal definition of rape based on consent. They also voted on the first-ever EU-wide rules to protect cats and dogs, requiring them to be microchipped and registered. Additionally, MEPs debated ways to hold Russia accountable for attacks on Ukrainian civilians and considered joining an international commission for Ukraine claims. Other votes covered topics like trade with developing countries and climate emissions.

**Impact** – These decisions mark important progress in different areas. Defining rape by consent helps protect victims and improve justice. The new animal protection law will improve the welfare of millions of pets by ensuring better care and traceability. Efforts to hold Russia accountable support justice for victims in Ukraine. Together, these actions show the EU working on rights, safety, and fairness for people and animals.

**What's next step** – After the votes, Parliament will start negotiations with the EU Council to finalize the 2028-2034 budget. The legal definitions and animal protection rules will move toward becoming official EU laws. Parliament will also continue working on resolutions about Ukraine and vote on related proposals in the coming days.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament took key steps to strengthen legal protections, animal welfare, and justice efforts, shaping policies that affect millions across Europe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen adopte des lois sur le consentement, la protection animale et la justice pour l’Ukraine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Parlement européen se réunit pour débattre et voter sur plusieurs sujets importants qui concernent les citoyens et les animaux de l’Union européenne, notamment le prochain budget, des protections juridiques et la justice liée au conflit en Ukraine.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 28 avril, les députés européens (DE) ont voté un rapport proposant une définition claire du viol basée sur le consentement. Ils ont aussi adopté les premières règles européennes pour protéger les chats et les chiens, qui devront être identifiés par puce électronique et enregistrés. Les députés ont discuté de la responsabilité de la Russie pour ses attaques contre des civils ukrainiens et envisagé l’adhésion à une commission internationale pour les réclamations ukrainiennes. D’autres votes ont porté sur le commerce avec les pays en développement et les émissions de gaz à effet de serre.

**Impact** – Ces décisions représentent un progrès important. La définition du viol basée sur le consentement renforce la protection des victimes et la justice. La nouvelle loi sur les animaux améliorera le bien-être de millions d’animaux domestiques grâce à un meilleur suivi et des soins accrus. Les efforts pour rendre la Russie responsable soutiennent la justice pour les victimes ukrainiennes. Ensemble, ces mesures montrent que l’UE agit pour les droits, la sécurité et l’équité.

**Prochaine étape** – Après les votes, le Parlement entamera des négociations avec le Conseil de l’UE pour finaliser le budget 2028-2034. Les définitions légales et les règles sur la protection animale avanceront vers leur adoption officielle. Le Parlement continuera de travailler sur des résolutions concernant l’Ukraine et votera d’autres propositions dans les jours à venir.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a franchi des étapes clés pour renforcer les protections juridiques, le bien-être animal et la justice, influençant la vie de millions de personnes en Europe.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo avanza leyes sobre consentimiento, protección animal y justicia para Ucrania
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parlamento Europeo se reúne para debatir y votar sobre temas importantes que afectan a personas y animales en la Unión Europea, incluyendo el próximo presupuesto, protecciones legales y esfuerzos de justicia relacionados con el conflicto en Ucrania.

**Qué pasó** – El 28 de abril, los eurodiputados votaron un informe que solicita una definición legal clara de violación basada en el consentimiento. También aprobaron las primeras normas europeas para proteger a gatos y perros, que deberán ser identificados con microchip y registrados. Además, debatieron cómo responsabilizar a Rusia por ataques a civiles ucranianos y consideraron unirse a una comisión internacional para reclamaciones de Ucrania. Otros votos incluyeron comercio con países en desarrollo y emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero.

**Impacto** – Estas decisiones representan un avance importante. Definir la violación por consentimiento protege mejor a las víctimas y mejora la justicia. La nueva ley de protección animal mejorará el bienestar de millones de mascotas con un mejor cuidado y seguimiento. Los esfuerzos para responsabilizar a Rusia apoyan la justicia para las víctimas en Ucrania. En conjunto, muestran que la UE trabaja por los derechos, la seguridad y la equidad.

**Próximo paso** – Tras las votaciones, el Parlamento comenzará negociaciones con el Consejo de la UE para finalizar el presupuesto 2028-2034. Las definiciones legales y las normas de protección animal avanzarán para convertirse en leyes oficiales. El Parlamento seguirá trabajando en resoluciones sobre Ucrania y votará propuestas relacionadas en los próximos días.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo dio pasos clave para fortalecer protecciones legales, bienestar animal y justicia, impactando a millones en Europa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a grand, open European parliamentary chamber rendered in warm, natural tones, featuring symbolic silhouettes of diverse animals—a microchipped dog and cat—gently positioned near abstract representations of balanced scales and interconnected puzzle pieces, illustrating unity, accountability, and new legislation for animal protection and justice within the EU budget framework.
